perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall continue for an initial term of year as of the Effective Date. This Agreement shall automatically be renewed for successive one (1) year increments unless either party request in writing, at least ninety (90) days prior to the anniversary date, that this Agreement not to be renewed.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall continue for an initial term of year as of the Effective Date. This Agreement shall automatically be renewed for successive one (1) year increments unless either party request in writing, at least thirty (30) days prior to the anniversary date, that this Agreement not to be renewed.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-renewal notice period from 90 days to 30 days can create enforceability issues if it contradicts established legal norms or reasonable business practices, particularly in jurisdictions with implied duties of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly a contradiction of a specific statute, reducing the notice period can be argued as violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, especially if it prejudices one party's ability to prepare for the termination of the agreement. Courts often interpret contracts in light of reasonable commercial practices and expectations, and a drastically shortened notice period could be deemed unreasonable.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the non-renewal notice period from 90 days to 30 days. While the provided law snippets do not explicitly define a specific notice period, snippet 1 refers to "§ 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith," stating that "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." The perturbation explanation and law explanation both suggest that drastically shortening the notice period may violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Therefore, reducing the notice period could contradict the obligation of good faith, potentially making the contract unenforceable. Given that this falls under Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, and is potentially unenforceable, the contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Contractor will supply all orders that do not exceed the forecast at the delivery times set forth in each Purchase Order. In the event Contractor anticipates at any time that it will not deliver Products within the prescribed timetable as set forth in the applicable Purchase Order, Contractor shall promptly so inform Customer by written notice of such delay. Contractor shall submit proposed revisions to the timetable that reflect Contractor's best estimates of what can realistically be achieved and shall use its best commercial efforts to achieve such timeline, unless otherwise directed by Customer and confirmed by Contractor.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor will supply all orders that do not exceed the forecast at the delivery times set forth in each Purchase Order. Contractor's sole responsibility is to inform the customer of any delays by written notice. No other actions are required from contractor to expedite delivery.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'best commercial efforts' clause and stating that only notification is required reduces the contractor's obligation, potentially conflicting with the Uniform Commercial Code's (UCC) implied duty of good faith and reasonable efforts in contract performance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - Duty of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: By explicitly stating that the contractor's *sole* responsibility is to provide notice, the revised text attempts to eliminate any implied obligation to take reasonable steps to mitigate delays or expedite delivery. This contradicts the UCC's requirement of good faith, which generally includes an obligation to act honestly and reasonably in the performance of a contract, especially when unforeseen circumstances arise.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the obligation of good faith as outlined in UCC § 1-304. By stating that the contractor's *sole* responsibility is to provide notice of delays, the revised text eliminates the implied obligation to take reasonable steps to mitigate delays or expedite delivery. This directly conflicts with the UCC's requirement that every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance.

perturbation.original_text: Amounts due hereunder may not be set off except with mutual prior written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: Amounts due hereunder may be set off at Contractor’s discretion, regardless of Customer consent.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the 'set-off' condition to allow contractor discretion is a potential issue. It might be permissible if explicit and not unconscionable but generally creditors must follow laws and procedures regarding debt collection and set-off.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts the general principles of fair debt collection and could run afoul of laws governing proper procedures for exercising set-off rights. While the FDCPA mainly applies to debt collection agencies, similar principles of fairness and transparency often apply to creditors seeking to collect debts, especially if the set-off provision is exercised in an abusive or deceptive manner. This is made more problematic in instances where one party has far less power than the other, as is frequently the case between manufacturers and suppliers.

perturbation.justification: The original text requires mutual prior written consent for set-offs, whereas the changed text permits set-offs at the contractor's discretion, irrespective of customer consent. This contradicts the principle of fair debt collection, as it potentially allows the contractor to unilaterally seize funds without adhering to standard legal procedures for debt recovery. While the provided snippets don't directly define 'set-off', the Law Explanation references principles of fairness and transparency applicable to creditors, suggesting the changed text undermines these principles. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references Subchapter V—DEBT COLLECTION PRACTICES and mentions sections related to 'unfair practices,' which could be relevant if the contractor's set-off discretion is abused.
